perturbation.original_text: 8 PRIVACY AND DATA PROTECTION 8.1 The Receiving Party undertakes to comply with South A frica’s general privacy protection in terms of the Bill of Rights (Section 14) in connection wit h this Proposal and shall procure that its personnel shall observe the provisions of such Act (as applicable) or any amendments and re- enactments thereof and any regulations made pursuant the reto.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 PRIVACY AND DATA PROTECTION 8.1 The Receiving Party will make reasonable efforts to comply with general privacy expectations.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit obligation to comply with South Africa's Bill of Rights (Section 14) regarding privacy, replacing it with a vague promise of 'reasonable efforts.'
perturbation.contradicted_law: Constitution of the Republic of South Africa, 1996, Section 14
perturbation.law_citation: Section 14 of the Constitution of the Republic of South Africa, 1996
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 14 of the South African Constitution guarantees the right to privacy. By weakening the clause to 'reasonable efforts,' the contract fails to ensure compliance with this constitutional right.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly requires compliance with South Africa's Bill of Rights (Section 14) regarding privacy. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'reasonable efforts,' creating a significant reduction in the Receiving Party's commitment to protecting privacy as guaranteed under Section 14 of the South African Constitution. This undermines the constitutional right to privacy, as the contract no longer ensures compliance, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The Company shall, within 7 (seven) days of receipt of a written demand from Transnet: a) return all written Confidential Information (including all copies); and b) expunge or destroy any Confidential Information from an y computer, word processor or other device whatsoever into which it was copied, read o r programmed by the Company or on its behalf. 3.4 The Company shall on request supply a certificate signed by a director as to its full compliance with the requirements of clause 3.3 (b) above.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 The Company shall, upon request, return all written Confidential Information. 3.4 Clause 3.3(b) is optional.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the timeframe for returning information and making clause 3.3(b) optional, the Receiving Party is not legally bound to expunge or destroy confidential information within a specific timeframe or provide certification of compliance. The deletion introduces legal risk, as the obligation to destroy information is weakened.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 (POPIA)
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA requires responsible parties (like the Company receiving confidential information) to destroy or delete records of personal information as soon as reasonably practicable after the purpose for which the information was collected has been achieved or is no longer authorised. The original clause enforces this by requiring destruction and certification; the modified version makes destruction optional, contradicting POPIA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the obligation to 'expunge or destroy' confidential information within a specific timeframe, and makes compliance with clause 3.3(b) optional. This contradicts the principle outlined in the law explanation, which references POPIA's requirement for responsible parties to destroy or delete records of personal information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 contains the following statement: 'The Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 aims: to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information'. By making the destruction of confidential information optional, the modified text undermines the 'minimum requirements for the processing of personal information' and weakens the protection of that information. Therefore, the omission creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Notwithstanding clause 2.1 above, the Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information: a) to those of its Agents who strictly need to know the Co nfidential Information for the sole purpose set out in clause 2.2 above, provided that the Receiving Party shall ensure that such Agents are made aware prior to the disclosure of an y part of the Confidential Information that the same is confidential and that they owe a duty of confidence to the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall at all ti mes remain liable for any actions of such Agents that would constitute a breach of this Agreement ; or
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Notwithstanding clause 2.1 above, the Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information: a) to those of its Agents. The Receiving Party is not liable for any actions of such Agents that would constitute a breach of this Agreement ; or
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'who strictly need to know the Confidential Information for the sole purpose set out in clause 2.2 above, provided that the Receiving Party shall ensure that such Agents are made aware prior to the disclosure of an y part of the Confidential Information that the same is confidential and that they owe a duty of confidence to the Disclosing Party' and 'The Receiving Party shall at all ti mes remain liable for any actions of such Agents that would constitute a breach of this Agreement' weakens protection of confidential information. This creates legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African common law duty of confidentiality
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles on Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_explanation: Under South African common law, a duty of confidentiality arises where information is disclosed in circumstances importing an obligation of confidence. By removing the Receiving Party's liability for its agents' actions, the agreement contradicts this common law principle, potentially allowing confidential information to be misused without recourse.

Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources . Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. Find sources: "Breach of confidence" – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR ( August 2013 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) This article is about breach of confidence in United States law. For breach of confidence in the law of England and Wales, see Breach of confidence in English law . turpi causa non oritur actio Joint and several liability Market share liability Transferred intent Remedies Damages Punitive Special Incidental Injunction Tracing Detinue Replevin Trover Other topics in tort law Tort reform Non-economic damages caps Quasi-tort Delict (term used for torts in some civil and mixed legal systems ) Conflict of tort laws Private attorney general Class action By jurisdiction Australia Canada China England and Wales European Union India Japan Taiwan United States Other common law areas Contracts Criminal law Evidence Property Wills , trusts , and estates Law portal v t e The tort of breach of confidence is, in United Kingdom law and the United States law , a common-law tort that protects private information conveyed in confidence . [ 1 ] A claim for breach of confidence typically requires the information to be of a confidential nature, which was communicated in confidence and was disclosed to the detriment of the claimant. Establishing a breach of confidentiality depends on proving the existence and breach of a duty of confidentiality. Courts in the United States look at the nature of the relationship between the parties. Most commonly, breach of confidentiality applies to the patient-physician relationship , [ 2 ] but it can also apply to relationships involving banks, hospitals, insurance companies, and many others. [ 3 ] There was no clear tort of breach of confidence in other common-law jurisdictions such as the United Kingdom or Australia ; however, there is an equitable doctrine of breach of confidence . In OBG Ltd v Allan [2007] UKHL 21 the Law Lords clarified that Economic torts in English law do exist. See also [ edit ] Abuse of information Misuse of private information Breach of confidence in English law United States free speech exceptions References [ edit ] ^ "Breach of confidence" . ^ Networks, Institute of Medicine (US) Committee on Regional Health Data; Donaldson, Molla S.; Lohr, Kathleen N. (1994). Confidentiality and Privacy of Personal Data . National Academies Press (US).
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Receiving Party's liability for the actions of their agents, which contradicts the principle that a duty of confidentiality arises when information is disclosed under circumstances that imply an obligation of confidence. The original text ensures agents are aware of their duty of confidence and holds the Receiving Party liable for breaches. By removing this liability, the agreement weakens the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the agreement contradicts common law principles. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states 'Establishing a breach of confidentiality depends on proving the existence and breach of a duty of confidentiality'. The removal of liability undermines this duty.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information ” means any information or other data relating to one p arty (the “Disclosing Party”) and/or the business carried on o r proposed or intended to be carried on by that party and which is made available for the purpos es of the Proposal to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) or its Agents by the Disclosing Par ty or its Agents or recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure and any other info rmation otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Agents to the Receiving Party or its Agents, whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement, and whether in writing or otherw ise, including any information, analysis or specifications derived from, containing or reflecting such information but excluding information which:- a) is publicly available at the time of its disclosure or becomes publicly available (other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party or any of its Agents contrary to the terms of this Agreement); or b) was lawfully in the possession of the Receiving Party or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable evidence) fr ee of any restriction as to its use or disclosure prior to its being so disclosed; or Non-Disclosure Agreement Transnet RFP / Agreement No…………………………… Page 4 of 7 c) following such disclosure, becomes available to the Rec eiving Party or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable ev idence) from a source other than the Disclosing Party or its Agents, which sour ce is not bound by any duty of confidentiality owed, directly or indirectly, to the D isclosing Party in relation to such information;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information ” means any information or other data relating to one party (the “Disclosing Party”) and/or the business carried on or proposed or intended to be carried on by that party and which is made available for the purposes of the Proposal to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) or its Agents by the Disclosing Party or its Agents. excluding information which:- a) is publicly available at the time of its disclosure; or b) was in the possession of the Receiving Party or its Agents prior to its being so disclosed; or c) following such disclosure, becomes available to the Receiving Party or its Agents from a source other than the Disclosing Party or its Agents;
perturbation.explanation: This change removes key elements of the definition of confidential information that protect the Disclosing Party. Specifically, it removes the following: 'recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure and any other information otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Agents to the Receiving Party or its Agents, whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement, and whether in writing or otherwise, including any information, analysis or specifications derived from, containing or reflecting such information' and 'other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party or any of its Agents contrary to the terms of this Agreement' and '(as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable evidence)' from conditions b) and c). This makes the definition far narrower, leading to legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African common law duty of confidentiality
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles on Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_explanation: The common law duty of confidence is broader than the narrowed definition provided in the modified text. The removal of 'recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure' means oral disclosures would no longer be protected even though under common law they would be. The removal of 'other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party or any of its Agents contrary to the terms of this Agreement' permits the receiving party to make something public and then claim it is no longer covered by the NDA. The removal of 'as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable evidence' makes it difficult for the disclosing party to prove the information fell under either exemption b) or c).
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing key protections for the disclosing party, creating a legal loophole. The perturbation explanation and the law explanation both highlight this weakening, indicating that oral disclosures and information made public by the Receiving Party would no longer be adequately protected. The removal of the evidence requirement (written records or other reasonable evidence) for exemptions b) and c) also weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to prove information falls under those exemptions. While no specific legal snippets were provided to directly assess a legal contradiction, the absence of these protections in the changed text, contrasted with the original text, significantly undermines the disclosing party's legal standing compared to common practices and reasonable expectations in NDAs, which the Law Explanation highlights.
